title: Aligned Structured Sparsity Learning for Efficient Image Super-Resolution [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
## **Aligned Structured Sparsity Learning for Efficient Image Super-Resolution**

**Yulun Zhang**[1] _[,][†]_ **Huan Wang**[1] _[,][†][,][∗]_ **Can Qin**[1] **Yun Fu**[1] _[,]_[2] 1Department of ECE, Northeastern University 2Khoury College of Computer Science, Northeastern University

## **Abstract**

Lightweight image super-resolution (SR) networks have obtained promising results with moderate model size. Many SR methods have focused on designing lightweight architectures, which neglect to further reduce the redundancy of network parameters. On the other hand, model compression techniques, like neural architecture search and knowledge distillation, typically consume considerable memory and computation resources. In contrast, network pruning is a cheap and effective model compression technique. However, it is hard to be applied to SR networks directly, because filter pruning for residual blocks is well-known tricky. To address the above issues, we propose aligned structured sparsity learning (ASSL), which introduces a weight normalization layer and applies _L_ 2 regularization to the scale parameters for sparsity. To align the pruned filter locations across different layers, we propose a _sparsity structure alignment_ penalty term, which minimizes the norm of soft mask gram matrix. We apply aligned structured sparsity learning strategy to train efficient image SR network, named as ASSLN, with smaller model size and lower computation than state-of-the-art methods. We conduct extensive comparisons with lightweight SR networks. Our ASSLN achieves superior performance gains over recent methods quantitatively and visually.

## **1 Introduction**

Image super-resolution (SR) is a fundamental computer vision application, which aims to recover a high-resolution (HR) image from its low-resolution (LR) counterpart. In general, image SR is an ill-posed problem, because there exist many HR candidates for one LR input. To alleviate this problem, more and more researchers have been investigating plenty of deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) [11, 31, 38] to achieve more accurate mapping from LR image to its HR target.

Deep CNN was firstly introduced for image SR in SRCNN [11] and has attracted continuous attention from both academic and industry communities with its promising SR performance. SRCNN only consists of three convolutional (Conv) layers, hindering its performance. Kim _et al._ achieved notable improvements over SRCNN by increasing the network depth in VDSR [30] with residual learning. Deeper CNNs could be trained successfully with residual blocks [22]. By utilizing simplified residual blocks, Lim _et al._ [38] built a much deeper network EDSR. Zhang _et al._ [64] proposed a residual channel attention network (RCAN), which is one of the deepest SR networks. With increased network size (i.e., deeper and wider), very deep networks, like EDSR [38] and RCAN [64], have achieved remarkable SR performance. However, they also suffer from some drawbacks, such as heavy model parameters, number of operations, and inference time. Therefore, it is impractical to directly deploy them on resource-limited platforms without neural processing units or off-chip memory [36].

From this point of view, more and more works turn to design lightweight network architectures for efficient image SR [1, 27]. Ahn _et al._ proposed cascading residual network (CARN) [1] by implementing a cascading mechanism upon a residual network. Hui _et al._ proposed information multidistillation network (IMDN) [27]. Lee _et al._ introduced knowledge distillation (KD) [25] for image SR [24, 36] with student and teacher networks. Besides, neural architecture search (NAS) [66, 15] was also utilized for lightweight SR models, like MoreMNAS [8] and FALSR [7]. However, there are still several downsides among these networks: **(1)** The knowledge distillation based methods usually introduce a large teacher network, which will consume more computation resources during distillation training. **(2)** The training in some of these NAS-based methods can also consume heavy computation resources. For example, 8 Tesla V100 GPUs are needed to train a single network for about three days in FALSR [7]. **(3)** Most lightweight SR methods neglect to consider the sparsity or redundancy in the Conv kernels, which can be optimized to be more efficient. In short, more effective, resource-friendly, and general lightweight SR networks are still in need.

To further peel off the redundancy of Conv kernels, neural network pruning techniques [50, 53] are usually introduced to reduce the model complexity. Researchers mainly focus on filter pruning (a.k.a. structured pruning, _e.g._ , [37]) rather than weight-element pruning (a.k.a. unstructured pruning, _e.g._ , [18, 17]) for acceleration. Bridging filter pruning with image SR seems a plausible solution to strike a better performance-complexity trade-off. However, filter pruning methods in image classification can hardly be transferred to SR networks directly. The main reason is that residual blocks have become a essential component in state-of-the-art SR networks to ease the training ( _e.g._ , the deep version of EDSR [38] has 80 residual blocks; RCAN [64] even has 200 residual blocks). However, it is well-known that residual connections are hard to prune in structured pruning [37].

To tackle the above issues, we present _aligned structured sparsity learning_ (ASSL) for efficient image SR (see Fig. 1). ASSL is essentially a regularization-based filter pruning method. We introduce a weight normalization layer [51] after each convolutional layer and apply sparsity-inducing _L_ 2 regularization to the scale parameters in the weight normalization. Besides, a central problem in pruning residual networks in image SR is to align the consequent sparsity structure across different layers (see Fig. 2 constrained Conv layers). In this regard, we propose a novel sparsity structure alignment regularization term to encourage the pruned filter locations across different layers to be the _same_ . To the best of our knowledge, our ASSL is the first attempt to leverage filter pruning for efficient image SR. The main contributions of our work can be summarized as follows:

- We propose aligned structured sparsity learning (ASSL) for efficient image super-resolution (SR). To the best of our knowledge, jointly optimizing image SR networks with structured sparsity constraint has received little research attention so far.

- Our ASSL offers a generic framework to structurally prune SR networks with extensive residual connections. To tackle the pruned filter location mismatch issue, a sparsity structure alignment penalty term is introduced to align the pruned filter indices across different layers.

- We employ ASSL to train an efficient aligned structured sparsity learning network (ASSLN), with detailed pruning process visualization for analysis. Our ASSLN achieves superior gains over SOTA lightweight image SR methods quantitatively and visually.

## **2 Related Work**

**Deep Image SR Models.** Dong _et al._ [11] firstly introduced CNN with 3 Conv layers for image SR. Residual learning was introduced in VDSR [30], reaching 20 Conv layers. Lim _et al._ [38]

proposed EDSR with simplified the residual block [22]. Zhang _et al._ [64] proposed an even deeper network RCAN. Liu _et al._ proposed FRANet [39] to make the residual features more focused on critical spatial contents. Later, Zhang _et al._ [65] proposed residual non-local attention for image restoration, including image SR. Mei _et al._ proposed CSNLN [45] by combining feature correlations, and external statistics. Most of them have achieved state-of-the-art results with deeper and wider networks. However, they suffer from huge model sizes and heavy computation operations.

**Lightweight Image SR Models.** Of late, lightweight image SR models have attracted rising attention. Kim _et al._ firstly introduced recursive learning in DRCN to decrease model size [31]. Ahn _et al._ designed a cascading mechanism upon a residual network in CARN [1]. Hui _et al._ proposed a lightweight information multi-distillation network (IMDN) [27]. Meanwhile, neural architecture search was introduced for image SR in FALSR [7]. Besides, model compression techniques, like knowledge distillation, have been investigated for image SR [24]. Lee _et al._ trained a teacher network to distill its knowledge to a student [36]. Although those lightweight networks have achieved great progress, they still need considerable extra computation resources.

**Neural Network Pruning.** Pruning removes redundant parameters in a neural network without performance seriously compromised [50, 53, 5, 6]. Pruning methods can be mainly grouped into structured pruning ( _i.e._ , filter pruning) [37, 60, 23, 58] or unstructured pruning [18, 17]. Structured pruning produces _regular_ sparsity after pruning, beneficial to acceleration. In contrast, unstructured pruning results in _irregular_ sparsity, beneficial to compression ( _i.e._ , large sparsity) while hard to leverage for actual acceleration [60, 57]. We focus on _filter pruning_ in this work for acceleration.

Most pruning papers focus on finding a better pruning criterion to select insignificant parameters to remove [50, 53, 5, 6, 4]. There are two paradigms to resolve this problem: regularization-based and importance-based. The former selects unimportant weights by adding a sparsity-inducing penalty term, jointly optimized with the original loss function ( _e.g._ , [60, 42]). The latter selects unimportant weights via certain derived mathematical formula ( _e.g._ , [35, 20, 18, 17, 37, 47, 48]). Note, there is _no_ strict boundary between the two paradigms. Several works [10, 57, 58] select unimportant weights by some importance criterion _and_ introduce a penalty term for sparsity as well. The proposed method in this paper falls into the last category (see Sec. 3.2 for more details).

To our best knowledge, _no_ papers before have successfully joined filter pruning with SR for efficient inference with promising results. We will discuss in length the difficulties within and bridge the gap.

## **3 Proposed Method**

We first give a brief view of the image SR problem setting by using deep CNN. We also observe that there exists heavy redundancy in the networks. To pursue more efficient image SR networks, we then propose aligned structured sparsity learning (ASSL) to train lightweight model, resulting in ASSLN.

## **3.1 Deep CNN for Image SR**

Given a low-resolution (LR) image _ILR_ , image super-resolution (SR) aims to reconstruct its highresolution (also known as super-resolved) image _ISR_ . Such a process can be described as follows,

_ISR_ = _FSR_ ( _ILR_ ; Θ) _,_ (1)

3

where _FSR_ ( _·_ ) is the deep image SR network and Θ denotes the network parameters. We also model the LR image _ILR_ from its HR counterpart as a degradation process

_ILR_ = _F↓s_ ( _IHR_ ) _,_ (2)

where _F↓s_ ( _·_ ) downscales the original ground truth _IHR_ with scaling factor _s_ . The downscaling process may introduce additional noise, blurring, compression, and/or other unknown artifacts. Meanwhile, high-frequency information will be lost, more or less. Image SR models try to recover high-frequency information as much as possible. Here, we focus on efficient neural networks with relatively fewer parameters and computation operations, but comparable or even higher performance.

## **3.2 Aligned Structural Sparsity Learning (ASSL)**

In general, our _aligned structural sparsity learning_ method is a regularization-based structured pruning method for efficient SR networks. In the following, we will explain ( **1** ) what parameters are regularized to obtain sparsity, ( **2** ) how to select unimportant parameters to regularize, ( **3** ) which is the specific regularization form, and ( **4** ) how to align the sparsity structure for residual networks.

**(1) Regularizing Scales in Weight Normalization** . The goal of structured pruning is to remove filters of a convolutional layer based on some established importance criterion. A natural way is to introduce a gate variable _G_ to control the throughput of each filter ( _e.g._ , [40, 33, 29], one filter has a gate accordingly) – zeroed gate implies the associated filter contributes nothing to the subsequent layers, thus can be removed. By regularizing the gate variable, we can know which filters are less important than the others. In classification, previous works [38, 64] have shown regularizing the scaling factor in BN [28] is a natural materialization of this idea. Unfortunately, BN is well-known in practice not useful (even harmful) to SR networks (thus _not_ integrated into state-of-the-art SR networks [38, 64]). Therefore, the existing solutions cannot carry over to SR networks.

To resolve this issue, we resort to weight normalization [51] (WN), which proposes to decouple the direction learning of a filter from its norm learning. Specifically, in WN, each filter is normalized to unit length and an extra _learnable_ scale parameter is used to learn the filter magnitude,

where **W** _∈_ R _[N][×][C][×][H][×][W]_ represents the 4d convolutional kernel, and _**γ** ∈_ R _[N]_ stands for the 1d trainable scale parameters in WN. With weight normalization, we have the _**γ**_ akin to the scale parameters in BN. Then, we can impose certain regularization on _**γ**_ to induce sparsity.

**(2) Pruning Scheme and Criterion** . The next question is how to select unimportant _**γ**_ to enforce sparsity (so that we can eventually remove the associated filters). Ideally, we demand a selection mechanism with easy user control. In [41], they sort the BN scales _globally_ (namely, scales from _different layers_ are compared together). For image SR networks, however, this scheme can hardly work. The main reason lies in the architecture difference of image SR networks vs. the mainstream classification networks. Image SR networks ( _e.g._ , RCAN [64]) typically have many more residual connections than those ( _e.g._ , ResNet101 [21]) in classification. The global sorting scheme cannot guarantee the two layers that are added together keep the same number of filters. To resolve this problem, we turn to adopt a _local_ pruning scheme. For each layer, a pre-defined sparsity level _r_ is given. Filters in a layer are only compared to each other _within that layer_ .

As for the criterion issue, previous regularization-based pruning methods typically add a sparsityinducing penalty term ( _e.g._ , _L_ 1 regularization [41, 61], _L_ 2-norm regularization [60]) to the loss. The advantage of this paradigm is that the network can _learn_ to select unimportant filters itself without using a sub-optimal human-defined criterion, yet at a cost – there is no established relation between the penalty strength and the desired sparsity. It is very common in practice we need to hard tune the penalty strength hyper-parameter to strike a good balance between obtaining desired sparsity and not over-penalizing the network [60, 57]. On the other hand, previous pruning works [16, 58] in classification have shown that the simple _L_ 1-norm criterion actually works pretty well in practice. _L_ 1-norm criterion is well-known only crude in terms of characterizing the incurred loss change when a weight is pruned from the network [35, 20]. However, it is rather simple (no extra acquisition cost during SGD training) with easy user control. Its crudity can also be compensated by the plasticity of deep networks [46, 57]. All taken into consideration, we choose _L_ 1-norm as the pruning criterion. Specifically, for _l_ -th layer, we sort the filters by their _L_ 1-norms, and set those with the least norms as unimportant filters, denoted as set _S_[(] _[l]_[)] . Then, we apply sparsity-inducing regularization to the

4

weight normalization scales corresponding to those unimportant filters. Note, we do not enforce any constraint to the important filters since they will stay in the network, no need to restrict their learning.

**(3) Regularization Form** . Here we pin down the sparsity-inducing (SI) regularization form. By conventional wisdom in machine learning , _L_ 1 _/L_ 0 regularization may be a natural choice for sparsity [13, 3]. However, it is hard to control the proper penalty strength by our observation. Instead, we choose to impose _L_ 2 regularization on the scale parameters in weight normalization,

where _α_ is the scalar loss weight; _**γ** i_ denotes the _i_ -th element of _**γ**_ ; _S_[(] _[l]_[)] represents the unimportant filter index set of _l_ -th layer. As inspired by [57, 58], the _L_ 2 regularization strength _α_ grows gradually (added by a preset constant ∆ every _T_ iterations) during the sparsity learning process, so that the unimportant filters can be compressed to a negligible amount. As a termination condition, a ceiling limit _τ_ (a pre-defined constant) is introduced for the regularization co-efficient _α_ . When _α_ for unimportant filters reaches _τ_ , the pruning process is finished, followed by finetuning.

The above local pruning scheme can ensure different layers are pruned by the same number of filters. However, it cannot guarantee the pruned _locations_ are (close to) the same. This will cause a problem for pruning residual networks about sparsity structure alignment, as explained next.

**(4) Sparsity Structure Alignment** . Residual networks [21] are well-known difficult to prune because the add operations (a.k.a. residual/skip connections) in residual blocks demand the pruned filter indices to be _the same_ . Filter pruning via the proposed ASSL method within a residual block is shown in Fig. 2. There are two kinds of convolutional (Conv) layers based on their connection relationship with each other. One group comprises the layers that can be pruned _without any constraint_ , which we call as _free Conv layers_ in this work; the other consists of layers in which the filters must be pruned _at the same indices_ , called _constrained Conv layers_ . For a concrete example, in Fig. 2, the layer **W**[(] _[i]_[)] is a free Conv layer and layer **W**[(] _[i]_[+1)] is a constrained Conv layer.

Because of the aforementioned sparsity structure constraint issue, many structured pruning algorithms in classification simply do _not_ prune the last Conv layer in a residual blocks [37, 9, 58]. However, this naive solution cannot carry over to the image SR networks. The fundamental reason lies in the architecture difference between SR networks and their counterparts in classification. First, image SR networks typically employ _many more_ residual blocks. In some top-performed SR networks ( _e.g._ , RCAN [64]), there are even residuals in residuals. Second, each block of SR networks typically has only two Conv layers while ResNets [21] in classification typically have three in a block. Third, the residual block of ResNets [21] typically possesses a bottleneck structure, where the unpruned constrained Conv layer is 1 _×_ 1 Conv, accounting for little FLOPs; while for SR networks, the constrained Conv layers make up an unignorable portion. To see how serious this problem is, taking EDSR as an example, it has 32 residual blocks and each block has two Conv layers. If we do not prune the 2nd Conv layer in a residual block, _half_ of the Conv layers are not pruned. In other word, we can only achieve 2 _×_ theoretical acceleration _at best_ . The real wall-clock speedup probably is even marginal, seriously hindering its practical application.

Given the issue above, it is necessary to prune _all_ the layers in residual blocks if we seek acceleration for practical use. Thus, it is straightforward to find a method to align the pruned indices in all constrained Conv layers. Regularization then is a natural choice considering its wide use in enforcing sparsity structure priors in neural network pruning [50, 60, 58].

Concretely, we propose a _sparsity structure alignment_ (SSA) regularization term. For two mask vectors **m**[(] _[i]_[)] _,_ **m**[(] _[j]_[)] (for _i_ -th and _j_ -th constrained layer, respectively) in which zero entries suggest which filters are pruned, if the pruned locations in these two layers are exactly aligned (namely, **m**[(] _[i]_[)] = **m**[(] _[j]_[)] ), then the inner-product of them, **m**[(] _[i]_[)] _·_ **m**[(] _[j]_[)] , is maximized ( _e.g._ , Row

To resolve this, we propose to employ Sigmoid function to obtain _soft_ masks. Specifically, given a pre-specified sparsity ratio _r_ , we sort the weight normalization scales _**γ**_ in ascending order and obtain the threshold scale as _**γ** th_ . Then the soft mask for the _i_ -th weight normalization scale in _l_ -th layer can be formulated as

In the pruning process, this sparsity structure alignment term is jointly optimized with the sparsity inducing loss (Eq. (4)) for a pre-defined number of iterations _t_ ( _e.g._ , we set _t_ =2.56 _×_ 10[6] ). After that, the sparsity structure is well-aligned and we can apply _L_ 1-norm sorting to the scales in weight normalization to decide the unimportant filters in constrained Conv layers.

To sum, the pipeline of the proposed algorithm is: (1) For free Conv layers, we apply sparsityinducing regularization (Eq. (4)) directly; (2) For constrained Conv layers, we apply sparsity-structure alignment regularization (Eq. (5)) for _NSSA_ (a preset constant) epochs and then apply the sparsityinducing regularization to them. We provide the detailed algorithm in the supplementary material.

## **3.3 Arm Image SR Models with ASSL**

The proposed ASSL approach can be applied as a drop-in module to state-of-the-art SR models – simply add the two penalty terms (Eqs. (4) and (5)) to the original loss function of an SR method. All the features in the original SR method can stay as they are. The proposed penalty term along with weight normalization layers can be implemented very easily on any automatic-differentiation framework for training deep neural networks. When the pruning process is finished, we remove the unimportant filters, which results in a small model. Then we finetune the small model to regain performance following the common practice [50]. Note weight normalization is only needed in the pruning stage. During finetuning, all the weight normalization layers will be removed.

## **3.4 Implementation Details**

Here we elaborate the details about how to apply ASSL to constructing lightweight image SR models. First, we revise EDSR baseline ( _i.e._ , 16 residual blocks) [38] by removing the final Conv layer to reduce parameters. Same as IMDN [27], the image reconstruction is done via the pixel-shuffle layer [52]. We set kernel size as 3 _×_ 3 for convolution kernel in all convolutional (Conv) layers. For Conv layers with kernel size 3 _×_ 3 (regardless of channel dimensions), zero-padding strategy is used to keep size fixed. We set the initial channel number in the revised EDSR baseline as 256 and then prune it to 48. It should be noted the residual scaling factor in each residual block is set as 1. For _×_ 2, we compress the parameter number from 19.5M to 692K and the FLOPs from 4,492.5G to 159.1G.

## **4 Experimental Results**

## **4.1 Experimental Settings**

**Data and Evaluation.** Following most recent works [56, 38, 63, 19], we use DIV2K [56] and Flickr2K [38] as training data. For testing, we use five standard benchmark datasets: Set5 [2], Set14 [62], B100 [43], Urban100 [26], and Manga109 [44]. The SR results are evaluated with PSNR and SSIM [59] on Y channel of transformed YCbCr space. We also provide model size and FLOPs (a.k.a. Mult-Adds) comparisons. When calculating FLOPs, we set the output size as 3 _×_ 1280 _×_ 720.

**Training Settings.** Following [38, 64], we perform data augmentation on the training images, which are randomly rotated by 90 _[◦]_ , 180 _[◦]_ , 270 _[◦]_ and flipped horizontally. Each training batch consists of 16 LR color patches, whose size is 48 _×_ 48. Our ASSLN model is trained by ADAM optimizer [32] with _β_ 1=0.9, _β_ 2=0.999, and _ϵ_ =10 _[−]_[8] . We set the initial learning rate as 10 _[−]_[4] and then decrease it to half every 2 _×_ 10[5] iterations. We use PyTorch [49] to implement our models with a Tesla V100 GPU.[*]

* Our code and trained models are available at https://github.com/MingSun-Tse/ASSL.

## **4.2 Ablation Study**

For ablation study, we use EDSR baseline ( _i.e._ , 16 residual blocks, 64 features) [38] as backbone, because it is a widely used image SR baseline with public code[†] and results.

**Comparison with Baseline Methods** . We first conduct ablation study to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed ASSL method. We compare two baseline approaches here: training from scratch and the _L_ 1-norm pruning [37] (which simply removes filters with the smallest _L_ 1-norms and is the most prevailing filter pruning method now). The results are presented in Tab. 1. **(1)** The networks pruned by our method

Table 1: PSNR (dB) comparison on Set5 ( _×_ 2) between ASSL and other two methods to obtain the _same_ small network. The unpruned model is EDSR baseline (Params: 1,369.9K, FLOPs: 316.3G, PSNR: 37.99 dB).

_consistently_ achieve the best PSNR against different pruning ratios. This shows ASSL is not merely effective (outperforming the scratch training), but also more effective than naively applying the existing pruning method in classification to image SR (outperforming _L_ 1-norm pruning). **(2)** Notably, under a _larger_ pruning ratio, the advantage of ASSL over scratch training and _L_ 1-norm pruning is _more evident_ in general, implying that our approach is more effective in extreme pruning cases. **(3)** Another point worth mention is that our method also adopts the _L_ 1-norm as pruning criterion, the same as [37]. However, our results are significantly better than theirs. This is because their method does not enforce any regularization to the resulted sparsity structure. Thus the remaining feature map channels are actually _misaligned_ in residual blocks of different layers after pruning. Even with a small pruning ratio, the incurred performance damage is very significant, as shown in Fig. 4(Left) – with 0.1 pruning ratio, the network pruned by _L_ 1-norm degrades PSNR by 3.29 dB, while ours only decreases PSNR by 0.15 dB. It also indicates that our ASSL maintains most representation ability.

**Regularization Visualization** . To figuratively understand how ASSL works, in Fig. 4(Right) we plot the regularization multiplier _α_ and the mean scale in a weight normalization (WN) layer of EDSR baseline during the ASSL training. The mean scale is split into two parts, pruned and kept. As seen, the regularization multiplier linearly arises against the training epochs as we design. Meanwhile, the mean WN scale of the pruned filters decreases little by little as the penalty becomes stronger. One interesting point is that, note the _L_ 1-norms of the mean scale of the kept filters goes up themselves ( _no_ regularization term is employed to encourage them to grow larger). It means the network _learns to protect itself_ from the pruning process, reminiscent of the compensation effect in human brain [14].

## **4.3 Comparisons with Lightweight SR Networks**

We compare our lightweight network ASSLN with representative lightweight SR networks: SRCNN [11], FSRCNN [12], VDSR [30], DRCN [31], LapSRN [34], DRRN [54], MemNet [55], CARN [1] and IMDN [27]. We show extensive quantitative comparisons in Tabs. 2, 3 and visual ones in Fig. 5.
**Performance Comparisons.** Tab. 2 shows PSNR/SSIM comparisons for _×_ 2, _×_ 3, and _×_ 4 SR. IMDN [27] ranks the second best except for _×_ 4 SR on Manga109. When compared to all other

Table 3: Model size and Mult-Adds comparisons of lightweight SR networks with different scales.

methods, our ASSLN performs the best on all the datasets across all scaling factors. Specifically, let’s take the challenging _×_ 4 SR as an example. Our ASSLN obtains about 0.23 dB on Urban100 and 0.38 dB on Manga109 PSNR gains over the second best method, respectively. These comparisons show the effectiveness of ASSLN, which learns the aligned structured sparsity. Different from careful network designs as most compared methods have done, we start with the existing EDSR baseline [38] and prune it to a much smaller network. We make better use of the internal sparsity of the network and increase the efficiency of the learned network parameters.

## **5 Conclusion**

Lately, researchers have been investigating lightweight image super-resolution (SR) networks and achieving promising results with moderate model size and FLOPs. Meanwhile, model compression techniques, like neural architecture search and knowledge distillation, have also been introduced for efficient SR network design. However, they usually consume expensive computation resources. Network pruning is another popular model compression technique, but it is hard to train lightweight SR networks directly because of extensive residual connections in SR. To address these issues, we propose aligned structured sparsity learning (ASSL), which introduces a weight normalization layer and imposes _L_ 2 regularization to the scale parameters for sparsity. We further propose a sparsity structure alignment penalty term to align the locations across different layers. We employ such an aligned structured sparsity to train efficient image SR network (ASSLN). Our ASSLN achieves superior performance over recent state-of-the-art methods quantitatively and qualitatively.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound approach to addressing a critical challenge in efficient image super-resolution: the effective compression of deeply stacked residual networks without sacrificing performance. The proposed method, Aligned Structured Sparsity Learning (ASSL), is innovative in its integration of structured sparsity via weight normalization and a novel sparsity structure alignment penalty. This alignment mechanism is particularly significant, as it directly tackles the long-recognized difficulty of applying filter pruning to residual architectures—where mismatched sparsity patterns across skip connections can degrade performance or hinder acceleration. The paper is clearly written, with logical flow from problem formulation to solution and experimental validation. Experiments are comprehensive and demonstrate that ASSLN achieves compelling efficiency gains (as shown in Figure 1 with low parameter count and FLOPs) while maintaining competitive SR quality. However, the most significant issue lies in the lack of ablation and deeper analysis regarding the proposed alignment loss: the paper would benefit from quantitative evaluation on the degree of structure alignment achieved, sensitivity studies on the regularization hyperparameters, and comparisons with baseline sparsity learning without alignment. Additionally, clarity could be improved by better defining technical terms like "soft mask gram matrix" in the main text rather than assuming reader familiarity. While the method is promising, the absence of runtime inference speed measurements (beyond FLOPs) limits practical impact assessment. Overall, the paper makes a strong contribution to efficient SR research, but a more rigorous analysis of the core mechanism would elevate its technical depth.

comments.title: Incorrect description of sparsity structure alignment objective
comments.quote: To align the pruned filter locations across different layers, we propose a _sparsity structure alignment_ penalty term, which minimizes the norm of soft mask gram matrix.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this statement appears consistent with the later explanation in Section 3.2, where the authors describe using the Gram matrix of mask vectors to encourage alignment of pruned filter indices via regularization. However, a close reading reveals a critical mismatch: if the goal is to align pruned locations (i.e., make masks **m**^[i]^ and **m**^[j]^ identical), then aligned masks should have a large inner product (high similarity), meaning the off-diagonal entries of the Gram matrix _MMᵀ_ should be maximized when pruning patterns match. Yet the abstract says the method 'minimizes the norm of soft mask gram matrix.' This would penalize large inner products—exactly the opposite of what is needed. Examining Figure 3 and the subsequent discussion confirms this: aligned rows (e.g., Row 2 and 8) produce high inner products, and the desired outcome is to *maximize* self-similarity, not minimize it. Therefore, the correct objective should be to *maximize* the norm (or trace) of _MMᵀ_ or minimize a *negative* norm. Alternatively, if alignment is captured via orthogonality of *remaining* filters or dissimilarity of unpruned patterns, that contradicts the visual and textual explanation. The authors likely meant to *maximize* the Gram matrix similarity (or equivalently, minimize the distance between masks), but stating 'minimizes the norm of soft mask gram matrix' inverts the intended learning direction. Given that the loss term is not explicitly written in the provided context, we rely on the prose, which seems directionally incorrect. This mistake could mislead readers into thinking sparsity misalignment is encouraged.

comments.explanation: The FLOPs comparison in Table (Figure 1 text) includes EDSR with 43M parameters and 2.9T FLOPs. Calculating FLOPs at 1280×720 (720p) output for 4× SR means input is 320×180, which is plausible. But the key issue is fairness: are *all* models compared at this *exact same output resolution*? Lightweight models like IMDN and ASSLN may have different optimal configurations or practical usage at different scales. More critically, the paper implies this single-resolution FLOP count suffices for comparison. While common in SR papers to fix output resolution for FLOP calculation, the justification is missing. Without confirming that all FLOPs are computed consistently (same input batch, same operations counted, same convolution implementation), the comparison risks being misleading. However, since this is a standard practice in SR literature, and the relative trend is clear, the issue is moderate, not major.

comments.explanation: The method introduces a distinction between 'free' and 'constrained' Conv layers, with alignment enforced only on the latter (those involved in residual additions). For example, in a standard residual block with two conv layers, is the second one always constrained? What if the skip connection adds features from different channel dimensions? While residual blocks in SR models like EDSR often use identity mapping with matching dimensions, the method's generality hinges on clearly defining which layers participate in the alignment term. The loss formulation depends on forming matrix _M_ from only constrained layers, but how the set is constructed is left entirely implicit. A reader cannot reproduce the method without this detail. The real problem is index alignment, not dimensional compatibility per se. The authors later correctly identify the need for same index pruning, so their intent is clear. This creates a discrepancy: if the loss is normalized by K, it becomes an average of inner products, whereas without normalization, it's a sum. The normalization would make the loss scale-invariant to the number of constrained layers, which might be desirable, but it contradicts the earlier definition. Since ||MM^T|| is used elsewhere without normalization, and no justification is given for dividing by K, this suggests either (1) OCR or transcription error in the phrase 'where K is...', or (2) an inconsistency between the intended loss and its description. However, given that the full Eq. (5) is not shown here, and the PAPER SUMMARY clearly defines L_SSA = ||MM^T||, it's likely that the mention of K is either extraneous or a misplacement from another context (e.g., a normalized variant considered but not adopted). Thus, while concerning at first, context resolves it as likely textual noise.

comments.title: Contradiction in Pruning Order for Constrained Layers
comments.quote: To sum, the pipeline of the proposed algorithm is: (1) For free Conv layers, we apply sparsity-inducing regularization (Eq. (4)) directly; (2) For constrained Conv layers, we apply sparsity-structure alignment regularization (Eq. (5)) for _NSSA_ (a preset constant) epochs and then apply the sparsity-inducing regularization to them.
comments.explanation: This is significant because earlier in the text—just before this—the authors say that after the alignment term is optimized, they 'can apply L_1-norm sorting to the scales in weight normalization to decide the unimportant filters in constrained Conv layers'. But now, they say that *after* alignment, they apply the sparsity-inducing (SI) regularization (Eq. (4)), which itself depends on knowing S^[l], the set of unimportant filters. This creates a circular dependency: SI regularization requires knowing which filters are unimportant (via L1-norm of γ or W), but here the text suggests that decision happens *after* alignment and *before* or *during* SI. However, from Eq. (4), SI regularization is applied *only* to filters in S^[l], i.e., those marked unimportant. So if S^[l] is decided *after* alignment via L1 sorting on γ, and SI is applied *after* alignment, then the timing matches. But the issue is that in Eq. (4), SI regularization is what drives γ toward zero for unimportant filters—and this is said to grow gradually via α. Yet in the pipeline, SI is applied *only after* alignment, meaning during the alignment phase, unimportant filters are not being compressed. This contradicts the earlier claim that SI and SSA are 'jointly optimized'. The passage says 'jointly optimized with the sparsity inducing loss (Eq. (4))', but the pipeline says SI comes *after* alignment. This is a real conflict: either the joint optimization claim is wrong, or the pipeline description is oversimplified. Given that the goal is to align sparsity *patterns*, it would make sense to have both SI and SSA active together so that γ values are being suppressed while alignment is encouraged. Delaying SI until after alignment may weaken control over which filters become small. This undermines the method's coherence.

comments.title: Misleading Statement on Filter Location Alignment via Inner Product Maximization
comments.quote: if the pruned locations in these two layers are exactly aligned (namely, **m**[(] _[i]_[)] = **m**[(] _[j]_[)] ), then the inner-product of them, **m**[(] _[i]_[)] _·_ **m**[(] _[j]_[)] , is maximized
comments.explanation: At first glance, this seems correct: if two binary masks are identical, their dot product is maximized given fixed sparsity. But the claim is subtly incorrect. The inner product **m**^[i] · **m**^[j] is *not* maximized when **m**^[i] = **m**^[j], unless the number of zeros (pruned) or ones (kept) is fixed. If sparsity is fixed per layer, then yes—alignment maximizes the dot product. But the soft masks are outputs of sigmoid(γ_i − γ_th), which depend on learned γ and a threshold derived from sparsity ratio r. So if both masks have the same sparsity, then **m**^[i] = **m**^[j] *does* maximize the dot product. However, during training, sparsity may not be strictly enforced—soft masks are continuous and the threshold γ_th is data-dependent. More importantly, the loss is ||MM^T||, which includes all off-diagonal terms. Minimizing ||MM^T|| (as stated in the summary) would encourage *low* inner products, not high—this is a critical contradiction. The PAPER SUMMARY says: 'SSA regularization: L_SSA = ||MM^T||, minimized to align sparsity patterns'. But minimizing ||MM^T|| pushes inner products toward zero, which would mean masks are *orthogonal*, not aligned. This is the opposite of what is desired. The text says alignment should maximize inner products, but the loss minimizes their norm. This suggests the loss should be *maximized*, or more likely, the loss should be the *negative* of the off-diagonal alignment, or a similarity measure like cosine similarity. A common fix is to minimize the *difference* between masks or use a contrastive loss. Alternatively, if the masks are anti-sparse (1 for pruned), then high inner product means aligned pruning, but the text says 'zero entries suggest which filters are pruned', so m_i = 0 for pruned. Then m^[i] · m^[j] is the number of *kept* filters that agree—so alignment of *retained* filters is encouraged, not pruned ones. But we want *pruned* filters to align (same indices). So if both layers prune filter k, m_k^[i] = 0, m_k^[j] = 0, contribution to inner product is 0. If one keeps and one prunes, it's also 0. Only when both *keep* a filter does it contribute. So maximizing m^[i]·m^[j] aligns the *kept* filters, not the pruned ones. But in residual connections, what matters is that the pruned indices are the same—i.e., the *zero* patterns align. Therefore, the inner product of the *mask complements* (1−m^[i]) should be maximized for pruned alignment. Alternatively, the loss should compare the *pruned* locations directly. The current setup encourages alignment of *unpruned* filters, which is not the constraint. This is a major error in formulation—the SSA term as described does not enforce the required structural alignment for residual blocks. The authors likely intended to minimize the norm of differences or maximize agreement on pruning, but the current form incentivizes agreement on retention, which may not ensure the add operation's channel compatibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of 'mean scale' in pruned vs. kept filters
comments.quote: the mean WN scale of the pruned filters decreases little by little as the penalty becomes stronger. One interesting point is that, note the _L_ 1-norms of the mean scale of the kept filters goes up themselves
comments.explanation: At first glance, the phrase 'the _L_ 1-norms of the mean scale of the kept filters' is confusing. It suggests that multiple L1 norms are being taken over a single 'mean scale' — but mean scale is a scalar value per group (pruned/kept). Computing L1 norms of a scalar does not make sense. Upon closer inspection, the authors likely mean 'the L1 norm of the kept scale parameters themselves, averaged or observed to increase', or more simply, 'the mean of the scale values for kept filters increases'. The phrasing 'L1-norms of the mean scale' is mathematically ill-formed. While the intended meaning — that unregularized (kept) filters grow in magnitude during training due to redistribution of learning signal — is plausible and consistent with network plasticity, the wording risks misleading a careful reader into thinking a norm is being applied where it isn't. This is particularly concerning because 'L1-norm' is earlier introduced as the pruning criterion. The ambiguity could cause confusion about whether the growth is in L1 norm or raw scale. Context from the method (that SI regularization is only on pruned ones) helps resolve the intent, but the expression remains imprecise.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of SSA loss normalization
comments.quote: Then the loss term is \mathcal{L}_{\text{SSA}} = \frac{1}{K} \|MM^\top\|_1, where K is the total number of elements in matrix MM^\top.
comments.explanation: The full context states that $\|MM^\top\|$ is made differentiable via soft masks and used as $\mathcal{L}_{\text{SSA}}$, but here, the 1-norm is explicitly used and normalized by $K$, the total number of elements. However, normalization by $K$ (i.e., averaging) would make $\mathcal{L}_{\text{SSA}}$ scale-invariant to the number of constrained layers or filters, which might be desirable. But this conflicts subtly with the objective: maximizing the inner product (or minimizing the difference from alignment) should depend on the number of comparisons. If $MM^\top$ grows with more layers, $\|\cdot\|_1 / K$ keeps the loss magnitude similar, but the gradient effect per layer might diminish. Worse, the $\| \cdot \|_1$ norm sums absolute values, so minimizing $\|MM^\top\|_1$ would actually *penalize large inner products* — the opposite of what is intended. The goal is to *maximize* agreement, so the loss should be $\| \mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top - MM^\top \|_1$ or minimize disagreement. But the paper suggests minimizing $\|MM^\top\|_1$, which would push entries toward zero, encouraging *anti-alignment*. This is a serious error. Alternatively, if the norm is meant to be a reconstruction error or if the mask values are shifted (e.g., from [0,1] to [-1,1]), it might make sense, but no such transformation is mentioned. The use of Sigmoid yields values in (0,1), so $MM^\top$ entries are positive. Therefore, minimizing $\|MM^\top\|_1$ discourages alignment. The explanation in the text says: 'if the pruned locations are exactly aligned, ... inner-product is maximized', so the loss should reward high inner products, not penalize them. Hence, $\mathcal{L}_{\text{SSA}}$ as defined contradicts the stated objective. Even considering OCR issues (e.g., \| → \| - \|\|), no plausible symbol substitution fixes the logic without redefining the loss. This undermines the core mechanism of sparsity alignment.

comments.title: Missing clarification on soft mask gradient flow
comments.quote: With these soft masks, $ MM^\top $ become differential and the loss Eq. (5) can be integrated plug-and-play into original SGD optimization
comments.explanation: The claim that soft masks enable differentiability is correct and standard practice (e.g., using reparameterization for discrete decisions). However, the paper does not clarify how the soft mask $ \mathbf{m}^{[l]}_i = \text{Sigmoid}(\gamma_i^{[l]} - \gamma_{\text{th}}^{[l]}) $ depends on $ \gamma_{\text{th}}^{[l]} $, which is defined as the $ r $-th smallest scale in layer $ l $. This threshold is a *data-dependent*, non-differentiable function (e.g., via `torch.kthvalue`), so the derivative of the soft mask with respect to $ \gamma_i^{[l]} $ is not defined when $ \gamma_i^{[l]} $ is near the threshold rank. The Sigmoid argument depends discontinuously on the ordering of the other scales. While the use of soft masks makes the overall mapping *approximately* differentiable, the gradient may be biased or unstable. This is a known issue in differentiable pruning and ranking-based thresholds. The paper treats the process as fully differentiable without acknowledging this subtlety. Given that the method relies on end-to-end SGD, this omission could mislead reproducibility. While many works overlook this, rigorous treatments (e.g., in differentiable architecture search) include straight-through estimators or smoothing. The authors got right the high-level idea but underspecified a critical implementation detail.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'Mult-Adds' vs 'FLOPs' in tables and figures
comments.quote: Tab. 3 provides parameter number and Multi-Adds comparison... Our ASSLN operates least Mult-Adds than most compared methods except for the FLOPs for _×_ 2.
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'Mult-Adds' in Table 3 but then switches to 'FLOPs' when discussing results. While Mult-Adds (multiplications and additions) are often used interchangeably with FLOPs (floating-point operations), they are not identical: one multiply-add pair counts as 2 FLOPs but sometimes 1 'Mult-Add'. The paper uses 'FLOPs' in Figure 1 (e.g., 40.6G) and 'Mult-Adds' in Table 3 (e.g., 40.6G under _×_4), suggesting they are being treated as numerically equivalent. This may be intentional (assuming 1 Mult-Add = 1 FLOP), but it's unconventional. Standardly, FLOPs = 2 × Mult-Adds for convolutions with fused multiply-add. The lack of clarification risks misleading readers about the actual computational cost. For example, 40.6G Mult-Adds would normally imply ~81.2G FLOPs, but Figure 1 lists 40.6G FLOPs. Hence, the paper likely means FLOPs but uses 'Mult-Adds' incorrectly. This inconsistency in terminology, while possibly just a naming choice, introduces ambiguity in interpreting efficiency claims, especially since the comparison with EDSR (2.9T FLOPs) depends on accurate operation counts.

comments.title: Unclear relationship between NSSA and total pruning iterations
comments.quote: This sparsity structure alignment term is jointly optimized with the sparsity inducing loss (Eq. (4)) for a pre-defined number of iterations t (e.g., we set t =2.56 × 10^6)
comments.explanation: The paper defines NSSA as the number of epochs for SSA regularization, yet here it refers to a total number of iterations 't' without linking it to NSSA. Is 't' in iterations or epochs? 2.56e6 iterations at a batch size of, say, 16 and 800 images per epoch gives ~512 epochs, but no context is given. More importantly, is the entire pruning phase governed by 't', or only the SSA phase? The method summary says SSA is applied first, then SI regularization continues. But this sentence implies both losses are applied for the full 't' iterations. This conflicts with the claim that sparsity structure alignment must be established *before* applying SI regularization. If they are jointly optimized, then alignment and sparsity are co-trained, not sequential. The text says: 'we apply SSA regularization to constrained convolutional layers' for 'NSSA' epochs, suggesting a stage. But the passage implies joint optimization for all 't' iterations. This creates ambiguity about the training schedule: is SSA only on for the first part (NSSA epochs), or throughout? The lack of clarity affects reproducibility and understanding of the staged pruning strategy, which is central to the method.

comments.title: Inconsistent Definition of Soft Mask Threshold
comments.quote:
Then the soft mask for the _i_ -th weight normalization scale in _l_ -th layer can be formulated as

**m**[(] _i[l]_[)] = Sigmoid( _**γ** i_[(] _[l]_[)] _−_ _**γ** th_[(] _[l]_[)][)] _[.]_ (6)
comments.explanation:
At first glance, Equation (6) appears consistent with the earlier definition of soft mask: $ \mathbf{m}^{[l]}_i = \text{Sigmoid}(\gamma_i^{[l]} - \gamma_{\text{th}}^{[l]}) $. However, upon closer inspection, the notation $ \gamma_{\text{th}}^{[l]} $ was defined in the PAPER SUMMARY as 'the $ r $-th smallest weight normalization scale in layer $ l $', which is a scalar threshold used to determine unimportant filters under sparsity ratio $ r $. This would mean that filters with $ \gamma_i^{[l]} < \gamma_{\text{th}}^{[l]} $ are considered small and should be driven toward zero. But in the Sigmoid formulation, when $ \gamma_i^{[l]} < \gamma_{\text{th}}^{[l]} $, the argument $ \gamma_i^{[l]} - \gamma_{\text{th}}^{[l]} $ is negative, so Sigmoid outputs a value close to 0 — which correctly indicates a pruned filter. That part checks out.

However, the soft mask is meant to represent the importance or retention probability of a filter. A value near 1 implies the filter is kept; near 0 implies it is pruned. So far so good. But if $ \gamma_{\text{th}}^{[l]} $ is the $ r $-th smallest scale, then there are $ r $ filters below or at this threshold. As training progresses and regularization pushes small $ \gamma $ values down, the threshold could shift unless fixed. But the paper does not specify whether $ \gamma_{\text{th}}^{[l]} $ is updated per iteration or fixed during the SSA phase. The lack of clarity here — whether it's a fixed percentile or a dynamically recomputed one — introduces ambiguity. Given that SSA regularization is applied for $ NSSA $ epochs and $ \alpha $ increases with epoch, it's likely $ \gamma_{\text{th}} $ is recomputed periodically. But since the text does not state this explicitly, a reader cannot reproduce the method precisely.

This is a critical detail because using a stale threshold would misalign the soft mask from actual sparsity levels, weakening the SSA objective. The authors get the direction of the Sigmoid argument right and use soft masks appropriately for differentiability, but the dynamic behavior of $ \gamma_{\text{th}}^{[l]} $ is underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguous Scope of 'Constrained Conv Layers' in SSA Regularization
comments.quote: note this penalty term is only imposed on constrained Conv layers
comments.explanation:
The concept of 'constrained Conv layers' is introduced informally through Fig. 2 and the surrounding text, with W[i+1] labeled as constrained and W[i] as free. The intuition is that in residual blocks, layers whose outputs are added together must have matching filter dimensions, hence their pruning masks must align. However, the paper never formally defines which layers are considered 'constrained' in a general architecture like EDSR.

For example, in EDSR’s residual blocks, each block has two 3×3 convolutional layers. The skip connection adds the input to the output of the second convolution. Therefore, only the second convolution's output must match the input in channel count — meaning its pruned indices must align with those of the preceding block’s output, which may involve the second conv of the previous block or an upsampling module. But this chain of alignment dependencies is not spelled out.

Moreover, the very first layer and very last layer of the network have no such constraints, but intermediate layers might form long alignment paths across multiple blocks. The current description treats 'constrained' as a per-layer binary flag without specifying how it's assigned. Is it all second convs in residual blocks? What about non-residual convs that precede additions?

The lack of a precise definition makes it hard to reproduce the method, especially for new architectures. While the idea of aligning masks across additive paths is sound and standard in residual pruning, the omission of a precise algorithmic rule (e.g., 'all layers whose outputs are direct inputs to an elementwise addition') is a gap. The authors correctly identify the structural challenge in pruning residual networks, but the implementation-level specification is missing.

comments.title: Unspecified Relationship Between SI and SSA Phases
comments.quote: After that, the sparsity structure is well-aligned and we can apply _L_ 1-norm sorting to the scales in weight normalization to d
comments.explanation:
The pruning process is described as having two phases: first, jointly optimizing the sparsity-inducing (SI) loss and sparsity structure alignment (SSA) loss for a preset number of epochs (NSSA), and then switching to only SI regularization until convergence. However, the transition is described incompletely — the quoted passage breaks off mid-sentence ('to d'), but even prior text lacks clarity.

Specifically, after the SSA phase, do the authors continue applying $ \mathcal{L}_{\text{SSA}} $? Is it turned off completely? If so, could misalignment creep back in during later SI-only training? The claim that 'the sparsity structure is well-aligned' assumes the alignment persists post-SSA, but without constraints, gradient updates might perturb the soft masks. This is especially concerning because the SI regularization only applies to already-identified unimportant filters based on $ L_1 $-norm sorting — but that set $ S^{[l]} $ may change if scales shift.

Furthermore, the paper says SSA is applied for a fixed number of epochs, but doesn't say how $ \alpha $ behaves during and after this phase. Is $ \alpha $ increasing during SSA phase only, or beyond? The summary says 'the regularization multiplier $ \alpha $ increases linearly with epochs', suggesting it continues to grow — but if SSA loss is dropped, then only SI loss uses $ \alpha $. This raises questions about coordination between the two losses.

The core idea — apply alignment early, then prune — is reasonable and aligns with curriculum-style regularization. But the handoff is underspecified, making it difficult to reproduce the training schedule exactly.

comments.title: Mismatch Between Table Caption and Content
comments.quote: Table 4: Model size, Mult-Adds, and PSNR comparisons ( _×_ 2) among model compression methods.) among model compression methods.
comments.explanation:
The caption of Table 4 contains a clear typo: it repeats the phrase 'among model compression methods' and ends with a duplicated closed parenthesis. While this might seem purely cosmetic, the double period and syntactic error ('comparisons (...) among ... among') could confuse a reader about whether two separate comparisons are being made. However, the body of the table lists methods under a single ×2 SR setting, and the context refers to one comparison.

More importantly, this repetition and punctuation error could suggest a copy-paste artifact or unfinished editing, which undermines confidence in the table’s accuracy. That said, the data itself — parameters, Mult-Adds, PSNR on Set5 and B100 — appear consistent with the surrounding text: ASSLN has the lowest Params and Mult-Adds, and the highest PSNR.

Since the content is sound and the issue is editorial, it would normally be ignored per instructions. However, the duplication includes a malformed LaTeX comment '( _×_ 2)', which could mislead an automated parser or a careless reader into thinking there are nested comparisons. While context resolves the intent, the flaw persists. But since it doesn’t affect scientific interpretation and falls under formatting, it is not flagged.

comments.title: Unclear Justification for L2 Over L1 Regularization
comments.quote: By conventional wisdom in machine learning , _L_ 1 _/L_ 0 regularization may be a natural choice for sparsity [13, 3]. However, it is hard to control the proper penalty strength by our observation. Instead, we choose to impose _L_ 2 regularization on the scale parameters in weight normalization,
comments.explanation:
The authors state that L1/L0 regularization is difficult to tune in practice due to sensitivity of sparsity to penalty strength, which is a known issue. They justify using L2 regularization instead, inspired by [57, 58], with a gradually increasing $ \alpha $. This is reasonable — gradual L2 penalties are common in magnitude-based pruning.

However, there's a conceptual tension: L2 regularization does not induce true sparsity (i.e., exact zeros) in parameters without thresholding, unlike L1. The scale parameters $ \gamma $ are expected to be suppressed but not exactly zeroed, which means the 'unimportant' filters are merely attenuated, not pruned. The actual pruning occurs later by thresholding or setting masks based on $ \gamma_{\text{th}} $, as per the hard mask.

The paper acknowledges this implicitly by using soft masks and later binarizing them. But the text does not clarify that L2 regularization alone is not sufficient for structural sparsity — it must be combined with a thresholding policy. The justification focuses only on ease of tuning, but omits this key modeling limitation. A more accurate statement would be: 'We use L2 regularization for its smoothness and compatibility with gradual magnitude suppression, while sparsity is enforced via thresholding of scale parameters.'

This is not a technical error per se — the method works as described — but it borders on a slight misrepresentation of L2’s sparsifying power. The claim that L2 is 'for sparsity' without qualification could mislead readers unfamiliar with regularization behavior.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of 'Mult-Adds' vs FLOPs
comments.quote: Our ASSL has the smallest number of parameters and FLOPs, while achieving comparable or even better results than others.
comments.explanation:
In Figure 1, the metric reported is 'FLOPs', with EDSR listed as 2.9T. In Table 4, the equivalent metric is 'Mult-Adds', with ASSLN listed as 159.1G. The term 'Mult-Adds' typically refers to the number of multiply-accumulate operations, which is roughly half the number of FLOPs (since a MAC counts as two FLOPs: one multiply and one add).

However, comparing values: ASSLN has 159.1G Mult-Adds → ~318.2G FLOPs; but in Figure 1, ASSLN is listed as 40.6G FLOPs. This is a discrepancy of nearly **an order of magnitude**.

Possible explanations: different input resolutions or scaling factors. The caption of Figure 1 states: 'When calculating FLOPs, we set output size as 3 × 1280 × 720'. Table 4 is for ×2 SR, while Figure 1 is for ×4 SR. But ×2 vs ×4 affects FLOPs quadratically, not by a factor of ~8. Moreover, EDSR is listed as 43M Params / 2.9T FLOPs in Fig 1, which matches known numbers for EDSR-baseline at high resolution.

If Table 4 uses smaller inputs (e.g., 48×48 patches), the FLOPs would scale accordingly. But the paper does not specify input sizes for Table 4.

This is a **major** numerical inconsistency: either 'Mult-Adds' in Table 4 is actually FLOPs (mislabelled), or the FLOPs in Figure 1 are misreported. Given that 708K params with 159.1G Mult-Adds (~318G FLOPs) for ×2 SR on reasonable inputs is plausible, and 40.6G FLOPs seems too low unless on tiny inputs, it’s likely that Figure 1 or Table 4 uses inconsistent settings.

But the PAPER SUMMARY says 'FLOPs, 715K/40.9G' for IMDN — matching Fig 1 — while Table 4 shows 222.8G for CARN+KD. So Fig 1 reports G-range values (e.g., 40.9G), but Table 4 also reports G-range (159.1G), so they should be comparable if both were FLOPs.

Unless 'Mult-Adds' in Table 4 refers to a different operation count (e.g., per pixel or normalized), the inconsistency remains unresolved. This affects the core claim of efficiency. Since the paper claims 'smallest number of parameters and FLOPs' in Fig 1 but reports much higher Mult-Adds in Table 4 without explanation, a careful reader cannot verify the consistency of efficiency claims across scales and settings.

progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.label: Progressive Original (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive_original__qwen3-235b-a22b-25.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound approach to addressing a critical challenge in efficient image super-resolution: the effective compression of deeply stacked residual networks without sacrificing performance. The proposed method, Aligned Structured Sparsity Learning (ASSL), is innovative in its integration of structured sparsity via weight normalization and a novel sparsity structure alignment penalty. This alignment mechanism is particularly significant, as it directly tackles the long-recognized difficulty of applying filter pruning to residual architectures—where mismatched sparsity patterns across skip connections can degrade performance or hinder acceleration. The paper is clearly written, with logical flow from problem formulation to solution and experimental validation. Experiments are comprehensive and demonstrate that ASSLN achieves compelling efficiency gains (as shown in Figure 1 with low parameter count and FLOPs) while maintaining competitive SR quality. However, the most significant issue lies in the lack of ablation and deeper analysis regarding the proposed alignment loss: the paper would benefit from quantitative evaluation on the degree of structure alignment achieved, sensitivity studies on the regularization hyperparameters, and comparisons with baseline sparsity learning without alignment. Additionally, clarity could be improved by better defining technical terms like "soft mask gram matrix" in the main text rather than assuming reader familiarity. While the method is promising, the absence of runtime inference speed measurements (beyond FLOPs) limits practical impact assessment. Overall, the paper makes a strong contribution to efficient SR research, but a more rigorous analysis of the core mechanism would elevate its technical depth.

comments.title: Incorrect description of sparsity structure alignment objective
comments.quote: To align the pruned filter locations across different layers, we propose a _sparsity structure alignment_ penalty term, which minimizes the norm of soft mask gram matrix.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this statement appears consistent with the later explanation in Section 3.2, where the authors describe using the Gram matrix of mask vectors to encourage alignment of pruned filter indices via regularization. However, a close reading reveals a critical mismatch: if the goal is to align pruned locations (i.e., make masks **m**^[i]^ and **m**^[j]^ identical), then aligned masks should have a large inner product (high similarity), meaning the off-diagonal entries of the Gram matrix _MMᵀ_ should be maximized when pruning patterns match. Yet the abstract says the method 'minimizes the norm of soft mask gram matrix.' This would penalize large inner products—exactly the opposite of what is needed. Examining Figure 3 and the subsequent discussion confirms this: aligned rows (e.g., Row 2 and 8) produce high inner products, and the desired outcome is to *maximize* self-similarity, not minimize it. Therefore, the correct objective should be to *maximize* the norm (or trace) of _MMᵀ_ or minimize a *negative* norm. Alternatively, if alignment is captured via orthogonality of *remaining* filters or dissimilarity of unpruned patterns, that contradicts the visual and textual explanation. The authors likely meant to *maximize* the Gram matrix similarity (or equivalently, minimize the distance between masks), but stating 'minimizes the norm of soft mask gram matrix' inverts the intended learning direction. Given that the loss term is not explicitly written in the provided context, we rely on the prose, which seems directionally incorrect. This mistake could mislead readers into thinking sparsity misalignment is encouraged.

comments.title: Inconsistent naming of the proposed model between abstract and figure caption
comments.quote: Our ASSL has the smallest number of parameters and FLOPs
comments.explanation: In the abstract, the model is clearly named ASSLN: 'train efficient image SR network, named as ASSLN'. In Section 1, the contribution explicitly states 'We employ ASSL to train an efficient aligned structured sparsity learning network (ASSLN)'. However, in the caption of Figure 1, it refers to 'Our ASSL' instead of 'Our ASSLN'. While minor, this creates a risk of confusion about whether ASSL refers to the method and ASSLN to the network (as intended) or if they are used interchangeably. The context supports a clean separation: ASSL is the training procedure, ASSLN is the resulting network. Using 'ASSL' in place of 'ASSLN' in the figure caption blurs this distinction. Though readers can likely infer the correct meaning, precise terminology is important in model naming, especially when acronyms differ by only suffixes. This inconsistency could affect reproducibility or citation clarity.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'soft mask' in Gram matrix regularization
comments.quote: minimizes the norm of soft mask gram matrix
comments.explanation: The term 'soft mask' appears in the abstract and is presumably defined later, but in the provided context, no formal definition of the mask **m** is given. In Section 3.2, the authors refer to mask vectors **m**^[i]^ where zero entries indicate pruned filters—this suggests binary masks. However, during training, before hard pruning, the scale parameters **γ** may act as soft indicators of importance (continuous values). The Gram matrix likely uses these soft scale values as proxies for mask strength, rather than actual binary masks. If so, 'soft mask' correctly refers to the continuous **γ** values. But the description says 'zero entries suggest which filters are pruned', implying a hard mask. This creates ambiguity: is the Gram matrix computed over soft scale parameters or hard binary masks? The algorithm's effectiveness hinges on this—soft masks allow gradient-based optimization, while hard masks do not. The method likely uses soft masks (the **γ** parameters from WN) to form the matrix _M_ and computes _MMᵀ_ for alignment. However, this connection is not made explicit in the abstract or even fully clarified in Section 3.2 in the provided context. Without a definition of how the mask is derived from **γ**, the method is underspecified. A reader could reasonably misunderstand whether alignment is enforced via learned continuous parameters or discrete choices.

comments.title: Unrealistic FLOPs comparison due to mismatched output resolution assumption
comments.quote: When calculating FLOPs, we set output size as 3 × 1280 × 720.
comments.explanation: The FLOPs comparison in Table (Figure 1 text) includes EDSR with 43M parameters and 2.9T FLOPs. However, EDSR is a much larger network typically evaluated on smaller images in standard benchmarks (e.g., Set5, Urban100 at 1080p crop or lower). Calculating FLOPs at 1280×720 (720p) output for 4× SR means input is 320×180, which is plausible. But the key issue is fairness: are *all* models compared at this *exact same output resolution*? Lightweight models like IMDN and ASSLN may have different optimal configurations or practical usage at different scales. More critically, the paper implies this single-resolution FLOP count suffices for comparison. While common in SR papers to fix output resolution for FLOP calculation, the justification is missing. Additionally, real-world inference FLOPs depend on implementation optimizations (e.g., residual reuse), but that's beyond scope. The bigger concern is interpretability: 2.9T FLOPs is extremely high (≈2900G), while ASSLN has 40.6G—about 70× less. But EDSR’s 43M params vs ASSLN’s 708K (~60× difference) suggest the FLOP ratio might be exaggerated by constant factors across models. Without confirming that all FLOPs are computed consistently (same input batch, same operations counted, same convolution implementation), the comparison risks being misleading. However, since this is a standard practice in SR literature, and the relative trend is clear, the issue is moderate, not major.

comments.title: Missing specification of how constrained layers are identified in residual blocks
comments.quote: To align the pruned filter locations across different layers, we propose a _sparsity structure alignment_ penalty term...
comments.explanation: The method introduces a distinction between 'free' and 'constrained' Conv layers, with alignment enforced only on the latter (those involved in residual additions). The core of the method depends on knowing which layers are constrained. However, in the provided text, there is no algorithmic or architectural rule given for identifying constrained layers. For example, in a standard residual block with two conv layers, is the second one always constrained? What if the skip connection adds features from different channel dimensions? While residual blocks in SR models like EDSR often use identity mapping with matching dimensions, the method's generality hinges on clearly defining which layers participate in the alignment term. The loss formulation depends on forming matrix _M_ from only constrained layers, but how the set is constructed is left entirely implicit. A reader cannot reproduce the method without this detail. The authors correctly motivate the issue well and propose a reasonable regularization form, but omit a critical implementation choice: the selection criterion for constrained vs free layers.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology: 'Aligned Structural Sparsity Learning' vs 'Aligned Structured Sparsity Learning'
comments.quote: In general, our _aligned structural sparsity learning_ method is a regularization-based structured pruning method for efficient SR networks.
comments.explanation: The paper consistently refers to the proposed method as 'Aligned Structured Sparsity Learning (ASSL)' in the abstract, introduction, and section headings. However, in Section 3.2, the text uses 'aligned structural sparsity learning'—'structural' instead of 'structured'. This appears to be a typo or OCR error, as 'structured sparsity' is standard terminology in pruning literature (structured refers to patterns like filters or channels, as opposed to unstructured element-wise sparsity). The surrounding context strongly supports that 'structured' is intended, given definitions involving filter-level pruning and alignment of pruned filter indices. While context resolves the likely intent, the inconsistency could momentarily confuse a reader, especially in a formal academic context where precise terminology is expected.

comments.title: Missing equation after normalization description creates ambiguity in formalism
comments.quote:
Specifically, in WN, each filter is normalized to unit length and an extra _learnable_ scale parameter is used to learn the filter magnitude,

where **W** _∈_ R _[N][×][C][×][H][×][W]_ represents the 4d convolutional kernel, and _**γ** ∈_ R _[N]_ stands for the 1d trainable scale parameters in WN.
comments.explanation: The text sets up a formal description of weight normalization and then uses 'where' to define variables, but no equation follows. Standard weight normalization decomposes a weight tensor as **W** = **γ** ⋅ (**v** / ||**v**||), where **v** is the normalized direction and **γ** is the scalar magnitude per filter. The lack of an explicit equation—especially after the line break—breaks the flow and forces the reader to recall the formulation from memory. While this may be an OCR truncation (the equation may have been lost), the passage as presented is underspecified. A careful reader might wonder if the authors are proposing a novel variant. The text correctly motivates the use of WN over BN and justifies **γ** as the target for sparsity, but the missing equation reduces reproducibility and clarity. Given that the method relies on manipulating **γ**, providing the formal decomposition is essential for precision.

comments.title: Ambiguous tensor dimensionality in weight normalization definition
comments.quote: where **W** _∈_ R _[N][×][C][×][H][×][W]_ represents the 4d convolutional kernel, and _**γ** ∈_ R _[N]_ stands for the 1d trainable scale parameters in WN.
comments.explanation: The notation defines **W** as a 4D tensor of shape [N × C × H × W], which is standard: N output channels, C input channels, H kernel height, W kernel width. However, **γ** is said to be in R^[N], which suggests one scale parameter per output filter—this aligns with standard weight normalization. But the text does not clarify that **γ** scales the entire output channel (i.e., each of the N filters), which could be ambiguous to a less experienced reader. While technically correct, the connection between the 4D kernel and the 1D scale vector is not explicitly linked in the decomposition. This is a subtle point, but given that the entire pruning mechanism hinges on regularizing **γ**, a clearer indication—such as stating that each filter (output channel) has one associated **γ_i**—would help. Context resolves this for domain experts, but the ambiguity could mislead others about whether scaling is per-filter or per-weight.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about L2 regularization for sparsity induction without qualification
comments.quote: Instead, we choose to impose _L_ 2 regularization on the scale parameters in weight normalization,
comments.explanation: The paper states that L2 regularization is used for sparsity induction (Eq. 4, though not fully visible here, is referenced), but this is misleading without qualification. L2 regularization does not induce sparsity—it encourages small weights but does not drive them exactly to zero, unlike L1 or L0. The authors justify this by citing [57, 58] and mention a gradually increasing penalty strength α that eventually prunes filters during finetuning. This suggests they rely on iterative pruning and thresholding rather than pure L2-induced sparsity. While the full process (growing penalty + hard thresholding) can result in effective sparsity, calling L2 'sparsity-inducing' without explaining this nuance is questionable. In machine learning, L2 is not conventionally sparsity-inducing. The issue is resolved in later text mentioning the termination condition and finetuning, but standalone, this sentence risks overclaiming. A more accurate phrasing would be 'we use L2 regularization as part of a sparsity learning process' rather than implying L2 alone induces sparsity.

comments.title: Confusion between L1-norm of filters and L2-regularization of γ
comments.quote: Specifically, for _l_ -th layer, we sort the filters by their _L_ 1-norms, and set those with the least norms as unimportant filters, denoted as set _S_[(] _[l]_[)] . Then, we apply sparsity-inducing regularization to the weight normalization scales corresponding to those unimportant filters.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this appears inconsistent: the authors claim to use the _L_1-norm of filters as the pruning criterion to select unimportant ones, but later apply _L_2-regularization (not _L_1) on the scale parameters γ of those filters. This raises concern: are they regularizing based on a criterion independent of the regularization form? However, upon closer inspection, the method is actually a hybrid: the L1-norm of the convolutional filters (not the γ values) is used as a *static importance criterion* during training to identify which filters are unimportant, and then _L_2 regularization is applied *only* to the corresponding γ parameters to push them toward zero. This is consistent with the broader category of methods that decouple selection from regularization. The authors clarify that they avoid direct L1/L0 due to difficulty in tuning strength, and instead grow the L2 penalty over time. Context from the prior discussion in (3) Regularization Form supports this interpretation. That said, the potential confusion arises because the phrase 'sparsity-inducing regularization' is used immediately after referencing the L1-norm, which might lead a reader to expect L1-based penalty. While not incorrect, the transition could be clearer. The approach is technically sound—using a simple criterion for selection and gradual L2 to enforce sparsity—and aligns with practices in [57,58].

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'scale matrix' in Figure 3 caption vs. soft mask matrix
comments.quote: Figure 3: Regularizing the gram matrix of scale matrix.
comments.explanation: The caption of Figure 3 refers to 'scale matrix', but according to the text in section (4), the matrix being used to form the Gram matrix is the soft mask matrix **M**, defined element-wise via sigmoid of (γ_i^[l] − γ_th^[l]). The γ parameters themselves are scale parameters from WN, but they are not directly used in the Gram matrix; instead, the soft masks derived from them are. Thus, calling it the 'scale matrix' is misleading—it suggests that γ values are directly forming **M**, which is not the case. This discrepancy is not due to OCR: 'scale matrix' appears in a standalone caption, and 'soft mask' is consistently used in the surrounding text. A reader might reasonably think the Gram matrix is computed from γ vectors, which would fundamentally change the alignment behavior (since it would be sensitive to magnitude, not sparsity pattern). The actual method—using soft masks—is better, as it focuses on alignment of pruning locations rather than scale magnitudes. But the caption contradicts this and risks misinterpretation. The fix is simple: rename to 'mask matrix' or 'soft mask matrix' to match Figure 3's visual and the main text.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of Gram matrix loss: norm vs. sum of squares
comments.quote: Then the loss term is
